target.name: Bicyclic nonadiene 4a
target.motivation: The observed reaction pathway is unprecedented for photocycloaddition reactions of allenes to a benzene core. The increase in structural complexity is remarkable given the planar structure of the substrate.

success_criteria.free_text: Achieve high yields and diastereoselectivity for the bicyclic products, demonstrating the utility of the ortho photocycloaddition cascade.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-O bond in the ether linkage of the bicyclic product
key_disconnections.reasoning: The bicyclic product 4 is formed by the incorporation of an alcohol (solvent) into a photocycloaddition intermediate. This suggests a nucleophilic attack on an electrophilic intermediate, likely a cyclooctatriene formed after ring opening of an initial ortho photocycloaddition product.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bonds forming the bicyclic core
key_disconnections.reasoning: The core bicyclic structure is formed via a photocycloaddition reaction between 2-methoxyacetophenone and 1,1-dimethylallene, specifically an ortho photocycloaddition followed by a cascade.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy leverages a novel ortho photocycloaddition of 1,1-dimethylallene to 2-methoxyacetophenone, which initiates a cascade reaction. This cascade involves a thermally allowed disrotatory ring opening of the primary ortho photocycloaddition product to a cyclooctatriene intermediate, followed by nucleophilic trapping (e.g., by an alcohol solvent) to form the complex bicyclic structure. This approach allows for the rapid generation of structural complexity from simple starting materials.

thought.background: Photocycloaddition reactions are powerful tools for synthesis. Aromatic carbonyl compounds can undergo Paterno-Büchi reactions (at the carbonyl) or photocycloadditions at the arene core (ortho, meta, para). Allene photocycloadditions to acetophenone have been reported, with 1,1,3,3-tetramethylallene leading to oxetanes (Paterno-Büchi) and parent allene showing some benzene core addition.
thought.gap: The regioselectivity of allene photocycloadditions to aromatic carbonyl compounds, especially with unsymmetrical allenes, is not fully explored. The potential for dearomatization and complex structure formation via arene photocycloadditions is high but underutilized.
thought.decision: To explore the photocycloaddition chemistry of aromatic carbonyl compounds with 1,1-dimethylallene, which has two distinct olefinic bonds, to understand regiochemical preferences and potential for complex product formation. Expect to observe different photocycloaddition pathways depending on the aromatic substrate.

evidence.text_span: In the context of our work on the deracemization of acyclic allenes,7 we came across the photocycloaddition of acetophenone (1) to allenes. Seminal studies by Gotthardt, Steinmetz, and Hammond had revealed that the compound undergoes the expected Paterno−̀ Büchi reaction when irradiated with 1,1,3,3- tetramethylallene (2,4-dimethyl-2,3-pentadiene).8 The oxetane product with an exocyclic olefin was isolated in $2 9 \%$ yield (Scheme 1), and the product of a 2-fold acetophenone addition (not shown) was obtained in $3 8 \%$ . Remarkably, when parent allene was employed as the olefin component, Gotthardt and Hammond observed the addition of the compound to the benzene core of acetophenone as a side reaction.9 Although the product was isolated in low yield, the result indicated to us that it might be worth exploring the photocycloaddition chemistry of aromatic carbonyl compounds to allenes in more detail. In this work, we now disclose our first preliminary results obtained with 1,1- dimethylallene (3-methyl-1,2-butadiene) as the olefin component.

thought.background: Previous studies showed Paterno-Büchi with symmetrical allenes and some benzene core addition with parent allene. 1,1-dimethylallene offers regiochemical interrogation.
thought.gap: Determine the regioselectivity and efficiency of photocycloaddition between acetophenone and 1,1-dimethylallene.
thought.decision: To investigate the photocycloaddition of acetophenone (1) with 1,1-dimethylallene in various solvents and at different wavelengths. Expect to find optimal conditions for a clean reaction and identify the primary product.

outcome.observation: The only isolable product was a symmetric compound, identified as the para photocycloaddition product 2a. The reaction was faster at 300 nm but cleaner at 350 nm. An excess of allene was required.

evidence.text_span: In initial experiments performed with 1 at a wavelength λ of 300 and $3 5 0 ~ \mathrm { \ n m }$ in various solvents, it turned out that methanol provided the cleanest conversion. An excess of the allene was required to drive the reaction to completion. The reaction was faster at $3 0 0 ~ \mathrm { { n m } }$ but cleaner at $3 5 0 ~ \mathrm { n m }$ . Surprisingly, the only isolable product under these conditions was a symmetric compound with only two distinct olefinic protons in the $^ 1 \mathrm { H }$ NMR spectrum. The tetrasubstituted olefin resulting from the allene was still present, indicating that the unsubstituted allene double bond had been involved in the photocycloaddition. In agreement with our assignment, single-crystal X-ray analysis confirmed the product to be para photocycloaddition product 2a (Scheme 2; all ORTEP representations are shown with $5 0\%$ probability displacement ellipsoids).

thought.background: Acetophenone (1) yielded para photocycloaddition product 2a. This suggests a general para selectivity for acetophenone derivatives.
thought.gap: Determine if this para selectivity extends to other 4-substituted acetophenones.
thought.decision: To subject other 4-substituted acetophenones to the same photocycloaddition conditions with 1,1-dimethylallene. Expect to obtain similar para photocycloaddition products.

outcome.observation: Para photocycloaddition products 2b-2d were the only compounds isolated and characterized, with yields ranging from 50-58%.

evidence.text_span: Without further optimization, other 4-substituted acetophenones were subjected to the reaction in the presence of 1,1-dimethylallene. Irradiation was continued until no aromatic ketone could be detected by TLC. In all reactions, para photocycloaddition products $\mathbf { 26-2d }$ were the only compounds that could be isolated and characterized. If benzaldehyde was subjected to the same reaction conditions, the reaction was sluggish, and only products from reactions at the carbonyl group were detectable.

thought.gap: Investigate the scope of the reaction with benzaldehyde.
thought.decision: To apply the same photocycloaddition conditions to benzaldehyde. Expect to see similar reactivity to acetophenone.
thought.goal: Synthesize a para photocycloaddition product from benzaldehyde.

outcome.observation: The reaction was sluggish, and only products from reactions at the carbonyl group were detectable, not the desired arene photocycloaddition products.

evidence.text_span: If benzaldehyde was subjected to the same reaction conditions, the reaction was sluggish, and only products from reactions at the carbonyl group were detectable.

thought.background: Acetophenone derivatives showed para photocycloaddition. Benzaldehyde was unreactive towards arene photocycloaddition. The position of substituents on the aromatic ring might influence regioselectivity.
thought.gap: Investigate the photocycloaddition of 2-methoxyacetophenone, where the methoxy group is ortho to the carbonyl, to see if it alters the regioselectivity.
thought.decision: To employ 2-methoxyacetophenone (3) as the carbonyl compound under the established photocycloaddition conditions. Expect to observe a different reaction pathway due to the ortho-methoxy substituent.
thought.goal: Synthesize a novel photocycloaddition product from 2-methoxyacetophenone.

outcome.observation: The major product was bicyclic nonadiene 4a, isolated as a single diastereoisomer. The solvent (methanol) was incorporated into the product, indicating an ortho photocycloaddition initiated a cascade reaction.

evidence.text_span: The reaction course of the photocycloaddition dramatically changed when 2-methoxyacetophenone (3) was employed as the carbonyl compound (Scheme 3). Under the conditions applied for the conversion of 1 to 2a, the major product was bicyclic nonadiene 4a. The compound was isolated as a single diastereoisomer, and it was immediately evident that the solvent had been added to a photocycloaddition intermediate.

thought.background: 2-Methoxyacetophenone (3) undergoes ortho photocycloaddition followed by solvent incorporation (methanol).
thought.gap: Determine if other alcohols can also be incorporated into the product via the same cascade mechanism.
thought.decision: To perform the reaction of 2-methoxyacetophenone (3) with 1,1-dimethylallene in ethanol, isopropanol, and 2,4-dimethylpentanol as solvents. Expect to obtain analogous bicyclic products with different alcohol moieties.
thought.goal: Synthesize a series of bicyclic products (4b-4d) with various alcohol additions.

outcome.observation: The reaction proceeded analogously in ethanol (to 4b), isopropanol (to 4c), or 2,4-dimethylpentanol (to 4d) as the solvent. In all cases, the alcohol was incorporated into the product, and yields varied between 59% and 74%. The product of the reaction in ethanol turned out to be crystalline, and its structure was confirmed by single-crystal X-ray diffraction.

evidence.text_span: The reaction proceeded analogously in ethanol (to 4b), isopropanol (to 4c), or 2,4-dimethylpentanol (to 4d) as the solvent. In all cases, the alcohol was incorporated into the product, and yields varied between $5 9\%$ and $7 4\%$ . The product of the reaction in ethanol turned out to be crystalline, and its structure was confirmed by single-crystal X-ray diffraction.

thought.background: The ortho photocycloaddition of 2-methoxyacetophenone (3) leads to a bicyclic product via solvent incorporation, suggesting a reactive intermediate.
thought.gap: Propose a detailed mechanism for the formation of products 2 and 4, including the nature of the intermediates.
thought.decision: To propose a mechanism involving a triplet state, diradical intermediate 5, followed by cyclization to either para product 2a (for X=H) or ortho product 6 (for X=OMe). For X=OMe, 6 undergoes disrotatory ring opening to cyclooctatriene 7, which is then trapped by the alcohol. Expect to find evidence for these intermediates.

evidence.text_span: Mechanistically, we propose that the photocycloaddition of ketones 1 and 3 proceeds via their rapidly populated triplet state14 and starts with the addition of 1,1-dimethylallene to the carbon atom adjacent to the carbonyl group (Scheme 4). After intersystem crossing (ISC), resulting diradical intermediate 5 can immediately cyclize to para photocycloaddition product 2a for $\mathrm { X } = \mathrm { H }$ . This pathway seems to be disfavored for $\mathbf { X } = \mathbf { M e O } .$ , in the case of which addition to the ortho-carbon atom is the predominant cyclization mode. In contrast to 2a, the dimethyl substituents of the allene seem to be easier to accommodate within the newly formed ring than at the exocyclic double bond. Primary ortho photocycloaddition product 6 is unstable and undergoes a thermally allowed disrotatory ring opening to yield intermediate cyclooctatriene 7. At this stage, we postulate the attack of the alcohol (ROH) to occur, resulting in the formation of addition products 4.

thought.background: A mechanism involving cyclobutane 6 and cyclooctatriene 7 intermediates has been proposed for the formation of products 4.
thought.gap: Provide experimental support for the proposed intermediates 6 and 7.
thought.decision: To attempt to isolate cyclobutane 6 by performing the reaction at low temperature and to identify any byproducts that support the mechanism. Expect to find evidence for the transient nature of 6 and the reactivity of 7.

outcome.observation: Cyclobutane 6 was isolated when the reaction was performed in dichloromethane at -40 °C. Compound 8, a byproduct resulting from the addition of methanol to the enol ether double bond, was detected in the reaction performed in methanol, supporting the intermediacy of 7 and its reactivity.

evidence.text_span: Support for the intermediacy of cyclobutane 6 stems from its isolation, when the reaction was performed in dichloromethane at $- 4 0 ~ \^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ and from compound 8, which was detected as a byproduct in the reaction performed in methanol. The addition of methanol to the enol ether double bond stalls the reaction sequence, with the disrotatory ring opening pathway being inaccessible for 8.

thought.background: The cyclooctatriene intermediate 7 can be trapped by alcohols (solvents).
thought.gap: Explore if other nucleophiles, not necessarily solvents, can also trap intermediate 7.
thought.decision: To change the solvent to dichloromethane and introduce external nucleophiles such as 4,4-dimethylcyclohexanol, (+)-menthol, benzoic acid, 1-adamantanethiol, and neopentylamine. Expect to obtain new bicyclic products with different nucleophilic additions.
thought.goal: Expand the scope of the nucleophilic trapping of intermediate 7.

outcome.observation: Ether 4e was obtained in 59% yield with 4,4-dimethylcyclohexanol. With (+)-menthol, a moderate diastereoselectivity was observed (36% major, 22% minor). Benzoic acid yielded ester 9. 1-adamantanethiol yielded thioester 10 (29% yield), and neopentylamine yielded secondary amine 11 (58% yield). No other reaction pathways were observed.

evidence.text_span: Since we speculated that intermediate 7 could also be trapped by other nucleophiles, which would ideally not be used as the solvent, we changed the solvent to dichloromethane. An initial experiment was performed with 4,4-dimethylcyclohexanol, which confirmed that the alcohol was not required to be the solvent (Scheme 5). Ether 4e was obtained in $5 9\%$ yield after an irradiation time of 6 h. When $\left( + \right)$ -menthol was used as the nucleophile, a moderate diastereoselectivity (d.r., diastereomeric ratio) was observed in the putative cyclization step from 7 to 4f. The two diastereoisomers could be separated with the major diastereoisomer being isolated in $3 6\%$ yield and the minor diastereoisomer in $2 2\%$ yield. The structure assignment remains tentative due to the flexibility of the $\mathrm { O- }$ C linkage, which precludes strong nuclear Overhauser effect (NOE) contacts between the menthyl group and the bicyclononadiene entity (see the Supporting Information for details). Benzoic acid was used successfully to trap putative intermediate 7, resulting in ester 9 as the product. Given the oxidative power of photoexcited acetophenone,15 it came as a pleasant surprise that potential reductants such as 1- adamantanethiol and neopentylamine could also be employed in the photocycloaddition−cyclization sequence. Thioester 10 was isolated in $2 9\%$ yield, and secondary amine 11 in $5 8\%$ yield. Even in the less polar solvent dichloromethane, there was no indication of any reaction pathways besides the ortho photocycloaddition, previously observed in alcoholic solvents.

thought.background: 2-Methoxyacetophenone (3) undergoes ortho photocycloaddition. The position of the methoxy group seems crucial.
thought.gap: Investigate if 2-methylacetophenone, an analogous ortho-substituted ketone, shows similar reactivity.
thought.decision: To irradiate 2-methylacetophenone in the presence of 1,1-dimethylallene under the established conditions. Expect to see ortho photocycloaddition.

outcome.observation: A slow conversion was observed, and only the product of a para photocycloaddition was detected, not the desired ortho photocycloaddition cascade product.

evidence.text_span: Attempts to involve 2-methylacetophenone in a related sequence were not successful. Upon irradiation in the presence of 1,1-dimethylallene $\lambda = 3 5 0 ~ \mathrm { { n m } }$ ; MeOH; $t = 1 4 \mathrm { ~ h ~ }$ ), a slow conversion was observed, and only the product of a para photocycloaddition was detected.

thought.background: Acetophenones show either para or ortho photocycloaddition depending on substitution. Salicylic acid derivatives have shown intramolecular ortho photocycloaddition in previous work.
thought.gap: Investigate the intermolecular photocycloaddition of methyl 2-methoxybenzoate (12) with 1,1-dimethylallene.
thought.decision: To irradiate methyl 2-methoxybenzoate (12) with 1,1-dimethylallene in various solvents and at different wavelengths. Expect to observe diverse photocycloaddition products due to the different electronic properties.

outcome.observation: Product mixtures were obtained from which individual compounds could be identified. Products emanating from all three photocycloaddition modes (ortho, meta, and para) were identified. The primary para photocycloaddition product was likely hydrolyzed to ketone 13. Product 14 was tentatively assigned as a meta photocycloaddition product. Bicyclo[4.2.0]octa-2,7-diene cores (15 and 16) were observed, characteristic of consecutive products of an ortho photocycloaddition.

evidence.text_span: Salicylic acid derivative methyl 2-methoxybenzoate 12 was employed as a final arene substrate in the photocycloaddition to 1,1-dimethylallene (Scheme 6). Previous work on the intramolecular photocycloaddition of salicylic acid derivatives had revealed that an appropriately O-tethered alkene at the phenoxy oxygen atom adds ortho to the benzene core.16 From the structure of consecutive products, it was deduced that the initial attack occurred at the two substituted arene C1 and C2 atoms. In this case, the regioselectivity of the approach is likely dictated by the tether, which enforces the formation of a fiveor six-membered ring. We are not aware of studies of the intermolecular photocycloaddition to a salicylate derivative. Due to the blue-shifted absorption of ester 12 relative to ketone 1 and 3, irradiation experiments with 1,1-dimethylallene were not successful at $3 5 0 ~ \mathrm { n m }$ . At $3 0 0 \ \mathrm { n m }$ , there was no single distinct product to be identified in either of the solvents we studied (MeOH, MeCN, $\mathrm { C H } _ { 2 } \mathrm { Cl } _ { 2 } ,$ and hexafluoroisopropanol). However, the reactions in acetonitrile and dichloromethane delivered product mixtures from which individual compounds could be identified. Since all products are 1:1 photocycloaddition products and have roughly the same molecular mass, a yield can be given, even though they were not isolated in pure form. Remarkably, products emanating from all three photocycloaddition modes (ortho, meta, and para) 4 were identified. The primary para photocycloaddition product was likely an enol ether, which appears to be hydrolyzed during workup, resulting in the isolation of ketone 13. For product 14, no consecutive reactions were observed, and its structure was tentatively assigned based on NMR data, suggesting a meta photocycloaddition17 (see the Supporting Information for details). The bicyclo[4.2.0]octa-2,7-diene core observed in methyl esters 15 and 16 is characteristic for consecutive products of an ortho photocycloaddition. In analogy to intermediate 7 (cf. Scheme 4), we propose the formation of cyclooctatriene 18 from 17, which continues to react photochemically in a disrotatory $\left[ 4 \pi \right]$ photocyclization to regioisomeric products 15 and 16.

validation.significance: This study provides a new method for the dearomatization of aromatic carbonyl compounds via photocycloaddition reactions with 1,1-dimethylallene. It demonstrates diverse regioselectivity (para, ortho, meta) depending on the aromatic substrate and highlights a novel cascade reaction initiated by ortho photocycloaddition, leading to complex bicyclic structures with multiple exit vectors. The ability to incorporate various nucleophiles into the bicyclic products expands the synthetic utility of this approach.

source.title: Dearomatization of Aromatic Carbonyl Compounds by Photocycloaddition Reactions to 1,1-Dimethylallene.
source.authors: Oxenfart L, Zuber J, Strassner N, Bach T.
